items.evidence_text:
Client: Hi Alex. I’m recently divorced and I just want a clean solo plan—like, confirm I’m on track and not missing anything obvious.
Advisor: Totally. We’ll keep it structured and efficient. Let’s start high level: what’s your total household gross income per year right now, just you?
Client: All-in, my gross is 127250 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a couple different streams, right—bonus and business income?
Client: Yes. Bonus and business income—bonus is weekly and the business income is kind of ad hoc.

Client: Yes. Bonus and business income—bonus is weekly and the business income is kind of ad hoc.
Advisor: Okay. Switching to spending—what do your total monthly expenses run, roughly, for everything you’re covering yourself now?
Client: It’s pretty steady at 4800 a month.
Advisor: Thanks. And on debt—do you have any monthly debt payments at all? Credit cards, car, student loans—anything like that?
Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.

Client: Hi Alex. I’m recently divorced and I just want a clean solo plan—like, confirm I’m on track and not missing anything obvious.
Advisor: Totally. We’ll keep it structured and efficient. Let’s start high level: what’s your total household gross income per year right now, just you?
Client: All-in, my gross is 127250 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a couple different streams, right—bonus and business income?
Client: Yes. Bonus and business income—bonus is weekly and the business income is kind of ad hoc.

Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Nice. Let’s talk assets next—what you’ve built up and where it sits. About how much do you have in investable assets total?
Client: Total investable assets are 319700.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just noting that down. Can you walk me through what makes up that total? We can do it bucket by bucket.
Client: Sure. I have a taxable investment account on a retirement platform—169100.

Client: And I’ve got a private markets position at a brokerage—40400.
Advisor: Perfect, that helps. And you mentioned you do have a home as well—what are you using as the current value for your primary residence?
Client: I’m using 83550 for the primary residence value.
Advisor: Thanks. Just to be clear, that home value is separate from the investable assets total we talked about.
Client: Correct—separate.

Advisor: Hi Linda—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client: Hi Alex. I’m recently divorced and I just want a clean solo plan—like, confirm I’m on track and not missing anything obvious.
Advisor: Totally. We’ll keep it structured and efficient. Let’s start high level: what’s your total household gross income per year right now, just you?
Client: All-in, my gross is 127250 annually.

Advisor: Okay—thanks. And just to confirm your marital status as we’re building this plan: are you currently married, single, divorced…?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Understood. And you mentioned wanting guardrails—how would you describe your risk tolerance in plain English? Conservative, moderate, aggressive?
Client: Moderate.
Advisor: Perfect. That lines up with what you were saying about wanting growth but not big drawdowns.

Client: Correct—loans outstanding total is 0.
Advisor: Great. Let me pause one sec—just writing that down.
Advisor: So assets-wise we’ve got your investable accounts plus the home at 83550, and on the liability side it’s clean—no mortgage and no other debt. Anything I missed that you’d consider a loan, even if it’s small?
Client: No—nothing. It’s all 0 on the loan side.
Advisor: Perfect. Next time we’ll use that clean slate to set the guardrails you mentioned and then decide what to do with the taxable account, retirement accounts, cash, and that private markets position.

Advisor: Hi Linda—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client: Hi Alex. I’m recently divorced and I just want a clean solo plan—like, confirm I’m on track and not missing anything obvious.
Advisor: Totally. We’ll keep it structured and efficient. Let’s start high level: what’s your total household gross income per year right now, just you?
Client: All-in, my gross is 127250 annually.

Client: Yes, 37%.
Advisor: Perfect.
Advisor: Last admin-ish thing: when we work together, you’re engaging me through an RIA—registered investment advisor—structure. Does that match what you’re expecting from this relationship?
Client: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great. And just so I’m not leaving anything hanging from today: income is your weekly bonus and the ad hoc business income, assets are the taxable account and the 401k or IRA on the retirement platform, cash at the bank, the private markets position at the brokerage, plus the primary residence value, liabilities are all 0, and no protection policies—still right?

Client: Hi Alex. I’m recently divorced and I just want a clean solo plan—like, confirm I’m on track and not missing anything obvious.
Advisor: Totally. We’ll keep it structured and efficient. Let’s start high level: what’s your total household gross income per year right now, just you?
Client: All-in, my gross is 127250 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a couple different streams, right—bonus and business income?
Client: Yes. Bonus and business income—bonus is weekly and the business income is kind of ad hoc.

Client: No, I don’t have any protection policies right now.
Advisor: Okay. Last thing for today’s baseline: you said you’re in finance, so you probably already have a sense of this—do you know roughly what tax bracket you’re landing in?
Client: Yeah, I’m in the 37% bracket.
Advisor: Perfect. Next time we can dig into goals and guardrails—like what level of drawdown feels tolerable—then map contributions and an investment mix around that.
Client: That’s exactly what I want—guardrails and a plan I can stick to.

Client: The bonus is 54900 annually.
Advisor: Okay. And the other piece is the business income that comes in ad hoc—what annual figure should I anchor to there?
Client: Business income is 72350 annually.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And that still ties back to the total gross annual income you gave me earlier.
Client: Yes, it all rolls up to that.

Advisor: Linda, before we hop off—one last thing I want to make sure I’ve got right is your role in the plan. In the household, are you the primary decision-maker?
Client: Yes—primary. It’s just me making the calls now.
Advisor: Got it. And related to that, I want to confirm how we’re labeling you in our notes—are you okay being listed as Client 1?
Client: Yes, that’s fine—1.
